issues.description: The PATCH /customers endpoint (list all customers) has no authentication guard. The spec says 'Verify caller has admin or superAdmin role' but no @AdminAuth() or any auth decorator is applied. Any unauthenticated user can enumerate all customer accounts including emails and profile data.
issues.suggestion: Add @AdminAuth() decorator to the index method and pass the admin payload to the provider for authorization enforcement.

issues.description: The PUT /admin/products/:productId endpoint uses @AdminAuth() but the spec and docstring describe this as a seller-only operation ('Only the seller who owns the product can perform updates'). Using AdminAuth for a seller operation means sellers cannot update their own products, and admins can update any product without ownership checks — a privilege escalation / logic flaw.
issues.suggestion: Clarify the intended actor. If this is an admin endpoint, update the documentation. If it should be seller-only, replace @AdminAuth() with @SellerAuth() and enforce seller ownership in the provider.

issues.description: The PATCH /seller/shipments/:shipmentId/deliveries endpoint is decorated with @SellerAuth() but the docstring states 'Authorization: Customer actor only. The customer must be the owner of the order.' This is an actor mismatch — sellers are confirming delivery instead of customers, which could allow sellers to fraudulently mark items as delivered.
issues.suggestion: Replace @SellerAuth() with @CustomerAuth() if customers confirm delivery, or update the documentation if sellers are the intended actors and add proper ownership validation.

issues.description: The super admin registration endpoint is publicly accessible with no invitation code, secret key, or existing admin authorization required. Any user can register as a super admin by calling this endpoint, which is a critical privilege escalation path.
issues.suggestion: Protect the super admin join endpoint with a pre-shared secret, invitation token, or require an existing super admin to authorize the creation. At minimum, add an environment-variable-based bootstrap secret check.

issues.description: The session record stores ip from props.body.ip with fallback to the real IP (props.ip). This allows a client to spoof their IP address in the session record by providing a fake IP in the request body, undermining IP-based audit trails.
issues.suggestion: Always use the server-extracted IP (props.ip) for session records. Do not trust client-supplied IP values.

issues.description: Failed login attempts (invalid credentials, suspended/banned accounts) are not logged. This makes brute-force detection and security monitoring impossible.
issues.suggestion: Log failed authentication attempts with timestamp, IP address, and email (hashed or partial) to an audit log system.

issues.description: The guest join endpoint creates a new guest session on every call with no rate limiting. This could be abused to create unlimited guest sessions, exhausting database resources.
issues.suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP address on the guest join endpoint.

issues.description: Error handling uses console.log(error) throughout all controllers. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but does not provide structured logging for security monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Consider replacing console.log with a structured logger (e.g., Winston, Pino) that supports log levels, correlation IDs, and integration with monitoring systems.

issues.description: The provider retrieves admin promotion request snapshots for a customer but does not verify that the promotion request belongs to the authenticated customer. Any customer with a valid JWT can access any promotion request snapshot by guessing or enumerating UUIDs.
issues.suggestion: Add a check that the parent promotion request is associated with the authenticated customer before returning the snapshot, e.g., include a customer_id filter on the parent record lookup.

issues.description: The provider lists admin promotion requests for a customer but applies no customer-scoped filter on the query (no customer_id in whereInput). Any authenticated customer can retrieve all promotion requests in the system, including those belonging to other users.
issues.suggestion: Add a customer ownership filter to the whereInput, e.g., filter by the requesting customer's ID to ensure customers only see their own promotion requests.

issues.description: The function looks up an admin record using props.seller.id to check for super_admin grade, but the authenticated principal is a seller (SellerPayload), not an admin. A seller's ID is unlikely to match an admin record, but the logic conflates two different identity domains. If the admin table and seller table share UUIDs by coincidence, this could be bypassed or produce unexpected authorization results.
issues.suggestion: Use a dedicated admin authentication payload (AdminPayload) for this endpoint rather than SellerPayload, or ensure the role check is performed against the correct identity domain.

issues.description: The sort field from user input is used to construct a dynamic orderBy key via fieldMap lookup with a fallback to the raw field value (`fieldMap[field] ?? field`). If `field` is not in fieldMap, the raw user-supplied string is used directly as a Prisma orderBy key, which could cause unexpected query behavior or information leakage through error messages.
issues.suggestion: Remove the fallback to the raw `field` value. Only allow explicitly whitelisted sort fields and return a default or throw a validation error for unknown fields.

issues.description: The date filter checks `if (props.body.created_at_min !== null)` but does not check for `undefined`. If the field is undefined (not provided), `new Date(undefined)` produces an Invalid Date, which could cause silent query errors or unexpected filtering behavior.
issues.suggestion: Check for both null and undefined: `if (props.body.created_at_min !== undefined && props.body.created_at_min !== null)` before constructing the Date object.

issues.description: No audit logging is performed when a delivery is confirmed. Delivery confirmation is a financially significant state change that should be logged for traceability and dispute resolution.
issues.suggestion: Add structured audit logging when a shipment delivery is confirmed, including seller ID, shipment ID, and timestamp.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/382 files] [2 chunks, 18→14 issues] The codebase has two critical broken access control issues: the customer list and customer detail endpoints have no authentication guards, exposing all customer PII to unauthenticated users. Additionally, the super admin registration endpoint lacks any authorization gate, allowing anyone to create super admin accounts. Several actor mismatches exist between documentation and implementation (seller vs customer for delivery confirmation, admin vs seller for product management). The super admin session also accepts client-supplied IP addresses, undermining audit integrity. Authentication flows (JWT, password hashing) appear correctly implemented. The overall security posture is significantly weakened by the unauthenticated customer data exposure.
